topic: Geological History of the Grand Canyon
title: Geological Evolution of One of the World's Most Spectacular Landscapes

lesson.learn: The Grand Canyon is a world-renowned example of erosion, exposing approximately 1.8 million years (or roughly 75% of the Colorado River's total 2.4 million year carving history) of geological history through the exposure of over 40 distinct rock layers. The Colorado River has carved out the canyon at an average rate of 1 meter (3.3 feet) per 1,000 years.
lesson.simpler: The Grand Canyon reveals 1.8 million years of geological history through its exposed rock layers, providing valuable insights into the region's geological evolution. By studying the rock layers, we can understand the formation of the region and its transformation over time.
lesson.keyTakeaway: The Grand Canyon's geological history is revealed through the exposure of over 40 distinct rock layers, providing valuable insights into plate tectonics, climate change, and the formation of the region's landscapes. The Grand Canyon's formation is an example of a complex geological process shaped by millions of years of erosion and weathering.
lesson.deeperDive: The Grand Canyon's geological history dates back to the Precambrian Era, with the earliest rocks found at the Vishnu Basement Rocks having an age of approximately 1.75 billion years. The oldest exposed rock layer in the Grand Canyon is the Zoroaster Granite, which has an age of around 1.8 billion years. Each rock layer exposed in the Grand Canyon provides a unique window into the region's geological past. For instance, the limestone cliffs of the Kaibab Formation date back approximately 270 million years, while the Bright Angel Shale formation at the bottom of the canyon contains fossils of ancient sea creatures dating back to the Paleozoic Era around 250 million years ago.
lesson.funFact: The Vishnu Basement Rocks are among the oldest rocks exposed in the Grand Canyon, with an age of approximately 1.75 billion years.

people: 1. John Wesley Powell (American geologist and explorer who first mapped the Colorado River)
2. Clarence Dutton (US Geological Survey geologist who conducted early research on the Grand Canyon's geology)
3. Robert Sprigg (Australian geologist who studied the canyon's stratigraphy)
places: 2. Colorado River (the main force behind the canyon's formation)
3. Kaibab Formation outcrops (exposed limestone cliffs in the northwestern part of the park)
4. Vishnu Basement Rocks area (oldest rocks at the bottom of the canyon)
years: 1. 1.75 billion years ago - formation of Vishnu Basement Rocks
2. 270 million years ago - limestone cliffs of Kaibab Formation deposited
3. 250 million years ago - fossils of ancient sea creatures deposited in Bright Angel Shale formation
4. 6 million years ago - Colorado River begins carving out the Grand Canyon
5. 1967 - Grand Canyon National Park designated a UNESCO World Heritage Site
concepts: 1. Plate tectonics (continental drift and mountain building)
2. Geologic time scale (periodization of Earth's history)
3. Erosion (processes shaping our landscape over millions of years)
4. Stratigraphy (study of rock layers and their geological significance)
5. Fossil record (record of ancient life forms preserved in rocks)
